questions.explanation: Judul lukisan 'Impression, Sunrise' menjadi asal-usul nama gerakan Impresionisme. Teks menjelaskan bahwa nama tersebut awalnya dimaksudkan sebagai hinaan oleh seorang kritikus.

questions.questionText: The word 'fleeting' in paragraph 1 and 5 suggests that a moment is:
options: Eternal and unchanging.
Predictable and boring.
Lasting only a very short time.
Difficult to understand.
questions.explanation: 'Fleeting' berarti sekilas atau berlangsung sangat singkat. Ini menunjukkan bahwa setiap momen yang dialami manusia itu bersifat sementara dan tidak dapat diulang kembali.

questions.questionText: What was the art establishment's initial reaction to Impressionist work?
options: It was immediately hailed as a masterpiece of realistic detail.
It was criticized for appearing unfinished and amateurish.
It was ignored because the subjects were too noble.
It was seen as a continuation of ancient Greek traditions.
questions.explanation: Kritikus dan kalangan seni tradisional awalnya menolak karya Impresionis, bahkan mengejeknya sebagai 'unfinished sketches' (sketsa yang belum selesai) dan 'mere impressions' (hanya kesan).

questions.questionText: Based on the passage, what is the relationship between Impressionism and Post-Impressionism?
options: Post-Impressionism rejected everything the Impressionists had taught.
Post-Impressionism used Impressionism as a foundation for further experimentation.
The two movements were exactly the same and occurred at the same time.
Impressionism was a reaction against the radical ideas of Post-Impressionism.
questions.explanation: Teks menjelaskan bahwa Post-Impresionisme muncul ketika para seniman menggunakan fokus Impresionisme pada cahaya dan warna sebagai titik awal untuk eksplorasi yang lebih personal dan struktural. Jadi, Post-Impressionisme dibangun di atas fondasi Impresionisme.

questions.questionText: Which of the following can be inferred about the 'ordinary subject matter' mentioned in paragraph 3?
options: It was considered more important than historical subjects by the Academy.
It helped the general public relate more easily to the art over time.
It was only possible to paint these subjects indoors.
It was primarily used to mock the 19th-century middle class.
questions.explanation: Fokus pada kehidupan sehari-hari dan subjek 'contemporary and the common' membuat seni menjadi lebih mudah diakses dan diterima oleh masyarakat umum seiring waktu. Ini membantu publik terhubung dengan karya seni.

questions.questionText: The author's tone toward the legacy of Impressionism is:
options: Skeptical
Appreciative
Critical
Indifferent
questions.explanation: Nada penulis bersifat menghargai (appreciative). Penulis mengakui warisan Impresionisme karena telah mengajarkan bahwa seni tidak harus menjadi cerminan sempurna realitas, melainkan interpretasi subjektif dari momen.

passages.title: The Human Immune System: A Sophisticated Defense Force
passages.content:
The human immune system is a remarkably complex and highly integrated network of cells, tissues, and organs that work together to defend the body against a constant barrage of harmful pathogens, such as bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites. This defense force is not a single entity but a multi-layered system that operates with extraordinary precision to distinguish between 'self' (the body's own healthy cells) and 'non-self' (foreign invaders). When this distinction is maintained, the immune system protects us from disease; however, if it fails, it can lead to autoimmune disorders where the body attacks itself. Understanding the mechanics of this system is fundamental to modern medicine and the development of life-saving treatments like vaccines and organ transplant procedures.

The first line of defense is the innate immune system, which provides a rapid but non-specific response to any perceived threat. This layer includes physical barriers like the skin and mucous membranes, which prevent most pathogens from entering the body. If an invader manages to penetrate these barriers, the innate system activates specialized cells called phagocytes. These cells roam the tissues and 'eat' (engulf) foreign particles and debris. Chemical signals, such as cytokines, are also released to trigger inflammation—a process that increases blood flow to the site of infection, bringing more immune cells to the battle. The innate system is always active and ready to respond immediately, regardless of whether it has encountered the specific pathogen before.

If a pathogen survives the innate response, the body activates the adaptive immune system. This layer is far more specialized and possesses two unique characteristics: specificity and memory. Unlike the innate system, the adaptive system identifies specific features of an invader, known as antigens. It primarily relies on two types of white blood cells called lymphocytes: B cells and T cells. B cells produce antibodies—highly specialized proteins that bind to a specific pathogen and mark it for destruction or neutralize its ability to infect cells. T cells, on the other hand, can directly kill infected body cells or coordinate the overall immune response. This adaptive process takes several days to become fully effective during the first encounter with a new pathogen.

The most extraordinary feature of the adaptive immune system is its memory. Once the system has successfully defeated a specific pathogen, it creates 'memory cells.' If the same invader enters the body again, even years later, these memory cells recognize it instantly and mount a response so rapid and powerful that the person often never even shows symptoms of the disease. This principle of immunological memory is the scientific basis for vaccination. By exposing the body to a harmless version of a pathogen's antigens, vaccines train the immune system to create memory cells without the risk of the actual disease, providing long-lasting protection.

Despite its brilliance, the immune system is not infallible. Various factors can compromise its effectiveness, including chronic stress, poor nutrition, lack of sleep, and certain genetic conditions. Furthermore, some pathogens, such as the Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV), have evolved specifically to attack and disable the immune system itself. On the other end of the spectrum, an overactive immune system can lead to allergies, where the body mounts a massive response to harmless substances like pollen or dust. As researchers continue to map the intricate cellular dialogues that govern immunity, new therapies are being developed to boost the system's power against cancer (immunotherapy) and to dampen it in cases of severe inflammation, highlighting the immune system's central role in human health and longevity.

questions.questionText: What is the primary topic of the passage?
options: The history of vaccination in modern medicine.
The structural components and functions of the human immune system.
The specific ways viruses like HIV evolve to bypass defenses.
The comparison between human and animal immunity.
questions.explanation: Topik utama dari keseluruhan teks adalah deskripsi mengenai struktur (sistem bawaan dan adaptif) serta fungsi dari sistem kekebalan tubuh manusia dalam melindungi dari patogen.

questions.questionText: The phrase 'non-self' in paragraph 1 refers to:
options: The body's own healthy cells.
Foreign invaders like bacteria and viruses.
Rare genetic mutations.
Useful nutrients from food.
questions.explanation: 'Non-self' merujuk pada entitas asing atau penjajah (foreign invaders) yang harus dikenali oleh sistem imun, seperti bakteri, virus, dan jamur. Ini berkebalikan dengan 'self' (sel sehat tubuh).

questions.questionText: According to paragraph 2, what is a key feature of the innate immune system?
options: It provides a highly specific response to each virus.
It creates long-term memory cells for future infections.
It provides a rapid, non-specific response to any threat.
It is only activated after several days of infection.
questions.explanation: Sistem imun bawaan (innate) dicirikan oleh respon yang cepat namun tidak spesifik (non-specific). Ini berarti sistem ini merespons ancaman apa pun tanpa perlu mengenali patogen tersebut sebelumnya.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is considered a physical barrier of the innate system?
options: Antibodies
B cells and T cells
The skin
Memory cells
questions.explanation: Kulit dan membran mukosa disebutkan sebagai lapisan pertahanan fisik pertama (physical barriers) yang mencegah patogen memasuki tubuh. Pilihan lain adalah komponen dari sistem adaptif.

questions.questionText: The word 'engulf' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to:
options: Release
Surround and swallow
Ignore
Reproduce
questions.explanation: 'Engulf' (dilakukan oleh fagosit) berarti mengelilingi dan menelan atau memakan partikel asing. Ini adalah istilah yang tepat untuk mendeskripsikan tindakan fagosit dalam 'memakan' partikel asing.

questions.questionText: What makes the adaptive immune system different from the innate immune system?
options: It is less effective at killing pathogens.
it possesses the qualities of specificity and memory.
it is the very first layer of defense.
It does not use white blood cells.
questions.explanation: Perbedaan utama adalah sistem adaptif memiliki dua karakteristik unik: spesifisitas (mengenali antigen tertentu) dan memori (mengingat patogen sebelumnya). Sistem bawaan hanya memberikan respon cepat dan non-spesifik.

questions.questionText: The term 'antigens' in paragraph 3 refers to:
options: Protective proteins produced by B cells.
Specific features of an invader that the immune system identifies.
The chemical signals that trigger inflammation.
The body's own healthy organ tissues.
questions.explanation: Antigen adalah fitur atau penanda spesifik (seperti protein permukaan virus) dari invader yang dikenali oleh sistem imun adaptif. Ini memungkinkan sistem untuk merespons secara khusus terhadap patogen tersebut.

questions.questionText: How do B cells and T cells differ in their function according to the passage?
options: B cells kill bacteria, while T cells only kill viruses.
B cells produce antibodies, while T cells can directly kill infected body cells.
T cells produce vaccines, while B cells produce inflammation.
There is no difference; they perform identical roles.
questions.explanation: Sel B bertanggung jawab memproduksi antibodi untuk menandai atau menetralkan patogen. Sementara itu, sel T memiliki kemampuan untuk membunuh sel tubuh yang sudah terinfeksi atau mengoordinasikan respons imun secara keseluruhan.

questions.questionText: The principle of immunological memory explains why:
options: Pathogens are able to evolve faster than humans.
Vaccines can provide long-term protection against diseases.
Allergic reactions happen more often in children.
Stress doesn't affect the immune system at all.
questions.explanation: Prinsip memori imunologis adalah dasar ilmiah di balik vaksinasi. Dengan memberikan antigen yang tidak berbahaya, sistem imun dilatih untuk menciptakan sel memori, sehingga memberikan perlindungan jangka panjang terhadap penyakit tersebut.

questions.questionText: What does the author suggest about an overactive immune system?
options: It is the goal of all immunotherapy treatments.
It can lead to problems like allergies.
It makes a person immune to all known viruses.
It is only caused by lack of sleep.
questions.explanation: Teks menyatakan bahwa di sisi lain spektrumnya, sistem imun yang terlalu aktif dapat menyebabkan alergi, karena tubuh merespons secara berlebihan terhadap zat tidak berbahaya seperti serbuk sari atau debu.

passages.title: Plate Tectonics: The Dynamic Foundation of Earth
passages.content:
The theory of plate tectonics, finalized by scientists in the 1960s, is the unifying concept of modern geology. It describes the Earth's outer shell—the lithosphere—not as a single, solid piece of stone, but as a jigsaw puzzle of several large and many small plates that are in constant, slow motion. These plates sit atop a semi-liquid layer of the mantle called the asthenosphere, which allows them to glide at speeds usually measured in only a few centimeters per year—about the same rate that human fingernails grow. While this movement is nearly imperceptible on a human timescale, over millions of years, it has completely reshaped the surface of our planet, creating mountains, opening vast oceans, and significantly influencing the evolution of life itself.

The movement of tectonic plates is driven by intense heat from the Earth's core, which creates convection currents in the mantle. As hot material rises and cooler material sinks, it pushes and pulls on the plates above. The most dramatic geologic events occur at the boundaries where these plates interact. There are three primary types of plate boundaries: divergent, convergent, and transform. At divergent boundaries, plates move away from each other, allowing molten rock (magma) to rise to the surface and create new crust. This process is most visible at mid-ocean ridges, where the seafloor is literally spreading apart. Convergent boundaries, where plates collide, can lead to the subduction of one plate beneath another or the massive folding of crust to form mountain ranges like the Himalayas.

Transform boundaries represent a different kind of interaction, where plates slide horizontally past one another. The most famous example is the San Andreas Fault in California. At these boundaries, the plates often become 'locked' due to friction. Stress builds up over years or even decades until the rocks finally snap, releasing a massive amount of energy in the form of an earthquake. Unlike divergent and convergent boundaries, transform boundaries do not usually create or destroy crust, but they are responsible for some of the most destructive natural disasters in human history. By mapping these fault lines, geologists can better understand and prepare for the seismic risks faced by cities located near plate boundaries.

The concept of plate tectonics also provides a powerful explanation for the distribution of fossils and similar rock formations across different continents. In the early 20th century, Alfred Wegener proposed the idea of 'continental drift,' suggesting that all the continents were once joined together in a single supercontinent called Pangaea. While he lacked a mechanism to explain how continents moved, plate tectonics provided the answer. By studying the magnetism of the seafloor and the ages of rocks across oceans, scientists confirmed that the continents have indeed been wandering for billions of years. This movement has not only altered climates but has also created isolated environments where unique species could evolve, highlighting the deep connection between geology and biology.

Today, plate tectonics is more than just a theoretical concept; it is an essential tool for understanding Earth's past and predicting its future. It helps us locate valuable natural resources like minerals and geothermal energy. It also offers insights into how Earth's climate has changed over geological time as continents moved into different temperature zones. As technology improves, scientists can now use satellite data to measure the precise movement of plates in real-time, further refining our model of the planet's dynamic interior. For humanity, living on a tectonically active planet means facing the risks of volcanoes and earthquakes, but it also means living on a world that is geologically 'alive,' constantly recycling its surface and maintaining a environment suitable for life.

questions.questionText: What is the primary focus of the passage?
options: The biography of Alfred Wegener and his travels.
The mechanics and consequences of plate tectonic theory.
The history of earthquake-resistant architecture in California.
The comparison between Earth's mantle and its inner core.
questions.explanation: Fokus utama teks adalah menjelaskan konsep teori tektonik lempeng, termasuk mekanisme penggeraknya (konveksi mantel) dan konsekuensinya di permukaan bumi.

questions.questionText: The word 'imperceptible' in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to:
options: Very fast
Noticeable
Unable to be seen or felt
Dangerous
questions.explanation: 'Imperceptible' berarti hampir tidak terasa atau tidak terlihat. Ini merujuk pada pergerakan lempeng yang sangat lambat sehingga sulit dideteksi dalam skala waktu manusia.

questions.questionText: According to the passage, what is the role of the asthenosphere?
options: It is the solid core of the Earth that produces heat.
It is a semi-liquid layer that allows tectonic plates to move.
It is the layer of the atmosphere where satellites orbit.
It is the underwater mountain range at divergent boundaries.
questions.explanation: Astenosfer adalah lapisan mantel yang bersifat semi-cair. Lapisan ini sangat penting karena memungkinkan lempeng tektonik untuk meluncur atau bergerak di atasnya.

questions.questionText: What force is cited as the primary driver of plate movement?
options: The gravitational pull of the moon.
Convection currents in the mantle caused by core heat.
The rotation of the Earth on its axis.
The pressure of the ocean water.
questions.explanation: Penggerak utama pergerakan lempeng adalah arus konveksi di mantel. Panas dari inti bumi menciptakan arus ini (material panas naik dan material dingin turun), yang mendorong dan menarik lempeng.

questions.questionText: Where does the creation of new crust typically occur?
options: At transform boundaries like the San Andreas Fault.
At convergent boundaries where mountains are formed.
At divergent boundaries where magma rises to the surface.
In the center of stable continents.
questions.explanation: Pembentukan kerak baru terjadi di batas divergen (divergent boundaries) karena lempeng bergerak saling menjauh. Proses ini memungkinkan magma dari mantel naik ke permukaan dan mendingin, membentuk kerak baru.

questions.questionText: The author mentions the San Andreas Fault as an example of:
options: A divergent boundary forming a new ocean.
A convergent boundary creating high mountains.
A transform boundary where plates slide past each other.
A stable region with no seismic activity.
questions.explanation: Patahan San Andreas adalah contoh batas transform. Di sini, lempeng bergerak secara horizontal melewati satu sama lain tanpa menciptakan atau menghancurkan kerak bumi secara signifikan.

questions.questionText: The word 'locked' in paragraph 3 refers to the fact that:
options: Plates are unable to move due to friction.
Scientists have completely understood the fault line.
The crust at convergent boundaries is very solid.
Magma is trapped beneath the surface forever.
questions.explanation: 'Locked' merujuk pada kondisi di mana lempeng terhenti gerakannya karena gesekan (friction). Gesekan ini membangun stres yang besar hingga akhirnya dilepaskan dalam bentuk gempa bumi.

questions.questionText: What was Alfred Wegener's contribution to geology mentioned in the text?
options: He was the first to measure plate movement with satellites.
He proposed the idea of continental drift and the supercontinent Pangaea.
He discovered the mid-ocean ridges.
He invented the machine used to study mantle convection.
questions.explanation: Kontribusi Wegener adalah mengusulkan teori 'hanyutan benua' (continental drift), yang menyatakan bahwa semua benua pernah bersatu dalam satu superbenua bernama Pangaea.

questions.questionText: What can be inferred about the connection between geology and biology?
options: Tectonic movement has no effect on the evolution of species.
The isolation of continents via plate movement allowed unique species to evolve.
Biology is more important than geology for understanding mountains.
All species evolved on a single continent that never moved.
questions.explanation: Dapat disimpulkan bahwa pergerakan lempeng menyebabkan isolasi benua. Isolasi ini menciptakan lingkungan yang berbeda-beda yang memungkinkan spesies unik berevolusi di setiap benua.

questions.questionText: The author describes the Earth as 'geologically alive' to suggest that:
options: The planet is a literal living organism.
The Earth's surface is constantly being recycled and reshaped.
There are more people living on Earth today than ever before.
Volcanoes are necessary for producing oxygen.
questions.explanation: 'Geologically alive' berarti bahwa permukaan bumi tidak statis. Ini mengacu pada proses dinamis di mana kerak bumi terus didaur ulang, dibentuk kembali oleh tektonik, dan mempertahankan lingkungan yang mendukung kehidupan.

passages.title: The Aurora Borealis: A Celestial Symphony of Light
passages.content:
The Aurora Borealis, commonly known as the Northern Lights, is one of the most breathtaking and mystifying natural phenomena on Earth. Characterized by vibrant, rhythmic displays of light that dance across the high-latitude night skies, this celestial spectacle has inspired countless myths and legends throughout human history. However, the scientific reality behind the aurora is equally fascinating, involving a complex interplay between the sun’s eruptive activity, the Earth's protective magnetic field (the magnetosphere), and the chemistry of our upper atmosphere. The phenomenon is not unique to the Arctic; a similar display, the Aurora Australis, occurs in the southern hemisphere.

The process begins millions of miles away on the surface of the sun. The sun constantly emits a stream of charged particles—primarily electrons and protons—known as the solar wind. During periods of high solar activity, such as solar flares or coronal mass ejections, the intensity of this wind increases significantly. As these high-speed particles reach Earth, most are deflected by the planet’s magnetosphere. However, near the North and South Poles, where the magnetic field lines converge and curve toward the surface, some of these charged particles are funneled into the upper atmosphere, particularly the ionosphere. This influx of energy represents a massive disturbance in the magnetospheric plasma, setting the stage for the light display.

Once the solar particles penetrate the atmosphere, they collide with ambient gas molecules, primarily oxygen and nitrogen. These collisions transfer energy to the atmospheric atoms, causing them to enter an "excited" state—a higher-energy level than their normal ground state. To return to their stable, lower-energy state, the atoms must release the excess energy in the form of photons, or particles of light. The specific color of the aurora depends on which gas is being struck and at what altitude the collision occurs. The most ubiquitous color, a pale yellowish-green, is produced by oxygen molecules located roughly 60 miles above the Earth. In contrast, rare all-red auroras result from collisions with oxygen at much higher altitudes, up to 200 miles, where the gas is thinner.

Nitrogen also plays a crucial role in the auroral palette. Collisions with nitrogen atoms often produce blue or purplish-red lights, which are typically seen along the lower edges of the auroral curtains. The structure and movement of the aurora—appearing as shimmering arcs, rippling curtains, or scattered patches—are dictated by the complexities of the Earth’s magnetic field lines. Because the charged particles follow these invisible paths, the aurora is constantly in motion, fluctuating in intensity and shape as the solar wind varyingly interacts with the magnetosphere. This dynamic nature makes every auroral event a unique, fleeting masterpiece of atmospheric physics.

The impact of these solar disturbances extends beyond visual beauty. Extremely powerful auroral events are often signs of geomagnetic storms, which can have significant effects on modern technology. These storms can induce electrical currents in power grids, potentially leading to widespread blackouts, and interfere with radio communication and satellite-based navigation systems (GPS). Consequently, space weather forecasting has become a vital field for protecting global infrastructure. Observing the aurora from the ground, especially during the long, dark winter months of the polar regions, offers not only a glimpse into the raw power of our sun but also a profound reminder of the delicate magnetic shield that makes life on Earth possible.

questions.questionText: What is the primary purpose of the passage?
options: To argue for better protection of the Earth's magnetosphere.
To explain the scientific mechanisms behind the Aurora Borealis.
To describe the historical myths associated with the Northern Lights.
To compare the Aurora Borealis with the Aurora Australis.
questions.explanation: Tujuan utama teks adalah menjelaskan secara ilmiah mekanisme fisika dan kimia yang mendasari fenomena Aurora Borealis (Northern Lights).

questions.questionText: The word 'ubiquitous' in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to:
options: Colorful
Rare
Found everywhere
Dangerous
questions.explanation: 'Ubiquitous' berarti ada di mana-mana atau sangat umum. Ini menunjukkan bahwa warna hijau kekuningan adalah warna aurora yang paling umum ditemukan.

questions.questionText: According to paragraph 2, why do auroras occur primarily near the North and South Poles?
options: Because the atmosphere is thicker at the poles.
Because solar winds are only directed toward the poles.
Because magnetic field lines converge and curve toward the surface there.
Because the poles are the coldest regions on Earth.
questions.explanation: Aurora terjadi di dekat kutub karena garis medan magnet bumi menyatu (converge) dan melengkung ke arah permukaan. Proses ini menyalurkan partikel bermuatan dari angin surya ke atmosfer.

questions.questionText: What happens when atmospheric atoms enter an 'excited' state?
options: They immediately release oxygen into the atmosphere.
They trap solar particles forever within the ionosphere.
They must release excess energy as light to return to a stable state.
They become part of the solar wind.
questions.explanation: Atom yang tereksitasi berada pada tingkat energi yang lebih tinggi. Untuk kembali ke keadaan stabil (ground state), atom tersebut harus melepaskan energi berlebih dalam bentuk foton (cahaya).

questions.questionText: Which of the following determines the specific color of an aurora?
options: The speed of the Earth's rotation.
The distance of the Earth from the sun.
The type of gas molecule struck and the altitude of the collision.
The time of year the aurora occurs.
questions.explanation: Teks secara eksplisit menyatakan bahwa warna aurora ditentukan oleh jenis molekul gas yang ditabrak dan ketinggian (altitude) di mana tabrakan tersebut terjadi.

questions.questionText: The author mentions 'low-altitude nitrogen' to explain:
options: Why some auroras appear pale green.
The cause of blue or purplish-red lights in the aurora.
Why auroras are only visible during the winter.
The source of energy for the ground-level atmosphere.
questions.explanation: Nitrogen menyebabkan cahaya biru atau merah-ungu. Teks menjelaskan bahwa ini biasanya terlihat di tepi bawah tirai aurora (low-altitude).

questions.questionText: The word 'induced' in paragraph 5 is closest in meaning to:
options: Prevented
Caused
Studied
Limited
questions.explanation: 'Induced' dalam konteks ini berarti menyebabkan atau membangkitkan. Jadi, badai geomagnetik 'menyebabkan' arus listrik.

questions.questionText: Based on paragraph 5, what is a potential negative consequence of high solar activity?
options: A permanent loss of the Earth's magnetosphere.
The creation of new, dangerous gases in the ionosphere.
Interference with satellite navigation and power grids.
An increase in the duration of polar winters.
questions.explanation: Aktivitas surya yang sangat kuat dapat memicu badai geomagnetik yang mengganggu jaringan listrik dan sistem navigasi berbasis satelit (GPS).

questions.questionText: What can be inferred about the visibility of the aurora?
options: It is impossible to see the aurora without specialized scientific equipment.
It is most easily observed when the sky is dark and the sun is active.
It occurs only when the Earth is closest to the sun in its orbit.
It is a static display that does not change its shape.
questions.explanation: Dapat disimpulkan bahwa aurora paling mudah diamati ketika langit gelap (musim dingin di kutub) dan aktivitas matahari sedang tinggi karena menghasilkan angin surya yang kuat.

questions.questionText: The author's attitude toward the aurora can best be described as:
options: Indifferent
Awestruck and informative
Skeptical
Fearful
questions.explanation: Penulis menunjukkan kekaguman ('breathtaking and mystifying') tetapi juga menyajikan penjelasan ilmiah yang rinci mengenai mekanisme fisika di baliknya.

passages.title: The Panama Canal: A Triumph of Engineering and Medicine
passages.content:
The Panama Canal, an 82-kilometer artificial waterway cutting across the Isthmus of Panama, stands as one of the most significant and challenging engineering feats in human history. By connecting the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans, the canal fundamentally transformed global maritime trade, allowing ships to avoid the treacherous and lengthy 13,000-kilometer journey around Cape Horn at the southernmost tip of South America. However, the successful completion of the canal in 1914 was not merely a matter of moving earth and stone; it was the result of a hard-won victory over unforgiving geography, immense engineering obstacles, and deadly tropical diseases.

The dream of a trans-isthmian canal began in earnest in 1881, when a French company led by Ferdinand de Lesseps, the diplomat behind the Suez Canal, initiated construction. However, the French attempt was plagued by disaster from the start. Unlike the relatively flat and sandy Suez, the Panamanian landscape was dominated by dense jungles, unstable mountains, and the volatile Chagres River. The French insisted on a sea-level canal, which required massive excavations of the Culebra Cut, a mountainous ridge. Frequent landslides and the onset of yellow fever and malaria decimated the workforce, claiming an estimated 20,000 lives. By 1889, the company went bankrupt, and the project was abandoned, leaving behind a scarred landscape and a legacy of failure.

In 1904, the United States, under the leadership of President Theodore Roosevelt, purchased the French assets and resumed work. Learning from the French mistakes, American engineers abandoned the sea-level plan in favor of a lock system. Central to this new design was the creation of Gatun Lake, a massive artificial body of water formed by damming the Chagres River. This lake served as a high-level bridge, significantly reducing the amount of excavation required across the isthmus. Ships would be lifted 26 meters above sea level by a series of locks, sail across the lake and through the Culebra Cut, and then be lowered back to sea level on the other side. This ingenious approach utilized Panama's geography rather than fighting against it.

Equally important to the canal's success was the eradication of disease. Colonel William Gorgas, the Chief Sanitary Officer, revolutionized tropical medicine by applying the recently discovered theory that mosquitoes were the primary vectors for yellow fever and malaria. Gorgas launched an unprecedented sanitation campaign, draining swamps, fumigating homes, and installing window screens across the Canal Zone. These efforts were met with skepticism by some officials, but they proved miraculously effective. By 1906, yellow fever had been almost completely eliminated from the region, allowing the massive labor force to focus entirely on the monumental task of construction.

When the Panama Canal finally opened on August 15, 1914, it reshaped the world's economic and strategic map. The 'Big Ditch' became a vital artery for international commerce, facilitating the movement of goods between the eastern and western hemispheres. In recent years, the canal has undergone a massive expansion to accommodate 'Neo-Panamax' ships—vessels significantly larger than those the original locks could handle. Today, the Panama Canal remains a testament to human resilience and ingenuity, illustrating how a combination of innovative engineering and groundbreaking medical science can overcome even the most daunting environmental challenges.

questions.questionText: What is the primary topic of the passage?
options: The biography of Ferdinand de Lesseps and his failures.
The economic history of the Republic of Panama.
The engineering and medical challenges overcome during the building of the Panama Canal.
The environmental impact of damming the Chagres River.
questions.explanation: Topik utama teks adalah tantangan ganda yang dihadapi dalam pembangunan Terusan Panama, yaitu aspek teknik rekayasa dan masalah medis (penyakit tropis).

questions.questionText: The word 'treacherous' in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to:
options: Fast
Dangerous
Long
Expensive
questions.explanation: 'Treacherous' berarti berbahaya atau berisiko tinggi. Ini menggambarkan rute pelayaran di sekitar Cape Horn yang penuh bahaya.

questions.questionText: According to paragraph 2, why was the French attempt at building the canal unsuccessful?
options: They lacked the financial support of their government.
They were unable to find enough workers to start the project.
They faced severe engineering issues and a high mortality rate from disease.
The United States prevented them from completing the work.
questions.explanation: Kegagalan Prancis disebabkan oleh masalah teknik (tanah longsor dan Culebra Cut) dan tingginya angka kematian akibat penyakit tropis seperti demam kuning dan malaria.

questions.questionText: Why does the author mention 'Culebra Cut' in paragraph 2?
options: To highlight a major excavation site that caused problems for the French.
To describe the location of the first successful medical research center.
To explain why the canal was built away from the mountains.
To name the place where the first lock system was installed.
questions.explanation: Culebra Cut disebutkan untuk menyoroti area pegunungan yang sangat sulit dan membutuhkan penggalian masif, yang menjadi sumber masalah teknik utama bagi Prancis.

questions.questionText: What was the primary difference between the French and American plans for the canal?
options: The Americans used only wooden locks instead of concrete.
The French wanted a sea-level canal, while the Americans implemented a lock system.
The French focused on Gatun Lake, while the Americans ignored it.
The Americans built a canal for trains instead of ships.
questions.explanation: Perbedaan mendasar adalah Prancis berencana membangun kanal sejajar permukaan laut (sea-level), sedangkan Amerika Serikat memilih sistem kunci (lock system) dengan memanfaatkan Danau Gatun.

questions.questionText: The word 'decimated' in paragraph 2 suggests that the workforce was:
options: Greatly increased in size.
Well-trained for tropical conditions.
Severely reduced in number by death or disease.
Relocated to the Suez Canal.
questions.explanation: 'Decimated' berarti dikurangi jumlahnya secara drastis. Dalam konteks ini, ini merujuk pada pengurangan tenaga kerja yang signifikan akibat kematian dan penyakit tropis.

questions.questionText: How did the creation of Gatun Lake assist in the construction of the canal?
options: It provided a source of electricity for the entire country.
It acted as a high-level bridge that reduced the need for excavation.
It drained the swamps where mosquitoes were breeding.
It served as a park for the canal workers.
questions.explanation: Danau Gatun berfungsi sebagai jembatan tingkat tinggi. Ini memungkinkan kapal dinaikkan di atas permukaan laut, sehingga secara signifikan mengurangi volume penggalian yang diperlukan di sepanjang daratan.

questions.questionText: Based on paragraph 4, what was William Gorgas's contribution to the project?
options: He designed the largest dam for the Chagres River.
He negotiated the purchase of the French assets.
He led a sanitation campaign that eliminated yellow fever.
He discovered a new way to excavate through mountains.
questions.explanation: Kontribusi terpenting Gorgas adalah menerapkan teori bahwa nyamuk adalah vektor penyakit. Ia memimpin kampanye sanitasi besar-besaran yang berhasil menghilangkan demam kuning dari wilayah tersebut.

questions.questionText: What can be inferred about the current status of the Panama Canal?
options: It is no longer useful for modern international trade.
It has been closed down due to modern environmental concerns.
It has been modernized and expanded to stay relevant for larger ships.
It remains exactly as it was when it first opened in 1914.
questions.explanation: Teks menyebutkan bahwa kanal telah mengalami perluasan besar-besaran untuk mengakomodasi kapal 'Neo-Panamax' yang lebih besar, menunjukkan bahwa kanal tersebut terus dimodernisasi dan relevan.

questions.questionText: The author's tone toward the building of the Panama Canal can best be described as:
options: Critical
Skeptical
Respectful and admiring
Melancholic
questions.explanation: Penulis menggunakan bahasa yang memuji pencapaian manusia dan menunjukkan rasa hormat ('triumph of engineering'), sehingga nadanya adalah penuh hormat dan kekaguman.

passages.title: The Evolution of Cetaceans: From Terrestrial Origins to Marine Giants
passages.content:
The evolutionary history of cetaceans—the group of marine mammals that includes whales, dolphins, and porpoises—represents one of the most stunning and well-documented transformations in the fossil record. For centuries, the origin of these massive creatures was a profound mystery, as they appear almost entirely alien to terrestrial life. However, modern paleontology and genetic analysis have confirmed that whales actually evolved from small, four-legged land mammals that walked on the Earth approximately 50 million years ago. This transition from a terrestrial existence to a fully aquatic one involved a dramatic series of anatomical and physiological adaptations that allowed cetaceans to thrive in the world's oceans.

The ancestors of whales are now known to belong to the Artiodactyla, an order of even-toed ungulates that includes modern-day hippopotamuses, cows, and deer. Genetic evidence suggests that the hippopotamus is the whale's closest living relative, sharing a common ancestor that lived on land. One of the earliest known cetacean precursors was Pakicetus, a small, wolf-sized mammal found in present-day Pakistan. While Pakicetus was predominantly a land animal, its ear bones already showed specialized features found only in whales, indicating an early adaptation for hearing underwater. This 'walking whale' likely hunted for fish in shallow streams, spending more and more time in the water to find food and escape predators.

As the millions of years progressed, intermediate species like Ambulocetus and Rodhocetus demonstrated the gradual shift toward a more aquatic lifestyle. Ambulocetus, often called the 'walking whale that swims,' had shorter limbs and large, webbed feet, similar to a modern sea lion or otter. It could still move on land, albeit clumsily, but was a powerful swimmer in coastal waters. Rodhocetus, appearing slightly later, showed even more specialized marine traits: its neck was shorter and more stable for swimming, and its tail began to grow more powerful. Significantly, the nostrils of these animals began to migrate from the tip of the snout toward the top of the head—the first step in the evolution of the blowhole.

By about 35 million years ago, cetaceans like Basilosaurus had transitioned almost entirely to the sea. Basilosaurus was a massive, serpentine creature that reached lengths of up to 18 meters. While it still possessed small, vestigial hind limbs that remained visible on the outside of its body, these legs were far too small to support its weight on land. This confirms that the transition was nearly complete; cetaceans had become obligate marine animals. Over time, their bodies became perfectly streamlined to reduce drag, their front limbs evolved into stiff flippers for steering, and their hind limbs eventually disappeared entirely, replaced by powerful tail flukes for propulsion.

The final major evolutionary divergence occurred about 30 million years ago, splitting cetaceans into two distinct suborders: the Odontoceti (toothed whales) and the Mysticeti (baleen whales). Each group developed unique biological tools for survival. Toothed whales, including dolphins and sperm whales, evolved a highly sophisticated system of echolocation to navigate and hunt in the dark depths of the ocean. Baleen whales, such as the blue and humpback whales, replaced their teeth with rows of fringed baleen plates made of keratin. These plates act as massive filters, allowing the whales to consume tonnes of tiny organisms, like krill and plankton, in a single gulp. This evolutionary journey from a small, terrestrial runner to the largest animals ever to live on Earth remains a powerful testament to the dynamic nature of life.

questions.questionText: What is the central theme of the passage?
options: The differences between toothed and baleen whales.
The historical mystery of whale migration patterns.
The evolutionary transition of cetaceans from land to sea.
The impact of climate change on modern whale populations.
questions.explanation: Tema utama teks adalah perjalanan evolusi (transisi) cetacea dari mamalia darat kecil menjadi mamalia laut raksasa seperti paus dan lumba-lumba.

questions.questionText: The word 'precursors' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to:
options: Modern descendants
Predators
Early ancestors or forerunners
Competitors
questions.explanation: 'Precursors' berarti leluhur awal atau pendahulu (early ancestors/forerunners). Dalam konteks ini, Pakicetus adalah prekursor cetacea.

questions.questionText: Based on the passage, which modern animal is most closely related to whales?
options: The shark
The hippopotamus
The elephant
The sea lion
questions.explanation: Teks secara eksplisit menyatakan bahwa kuda nil (hippopotamus) adalah kerabat hidup terdekat dari paus.

questions.questionText: What was unusual about the ear bones of Pakicetus?
options: They were much larger than those of any other land mammal.
They showed specialized features that are only found in whales.
They were completely missing from the fossil record.
They allowed the animal to breathe underwater.
questions.explanation: Tulang telinga Pakicetus menunjukkan fitur-fitur khusus yang hanya ditemukan pada paus, yang merupakan adaptasi awal untuk pendengaran di bawah air.

questions.questionText: Which of the following describes the movements of Ambulocetus?
options: It was an extremely fast runner on land.
It was a powerful swimmer but moved clumsily on land.
it could fly over the ocean to catch fish.
It spent its entire life on the deep ocean floor.
questions.explanation: Ambulocetus digambarkan sebagai 'walking whale that swims'. Artinya, ia adalah perenang yang kuat tetapi bergerak kikuk (clumsily) ketika berada di darat.

questions.questionText: The word 'migrate' in paragraph 3 refers to:
options: The seasonal travel of whales across the ocean.
The movement of early humans into whale territory.
The physical shifting of the nostrils toward the top of the head.
The process of land animals moving into the water for food.
questions.explanation: 'Migrate' di sini merujuk pada pergeseran fisik lubang hidung (nostrils) dari ujung moncong ke bagian atas kepala, yang merupakan langkah awal menuju evolusi blowhole.

questions.questionText: How does the author characterize Basilosaurus?
options: As a small fish-like creature with no bones.
As an early ancestor that still lived primarily on land.
As a massive, marine animal with small, useless hind legs.
As the first whale to develop baleen plates.
questions.explanation: Basilosaurus digambarkan sebagai makhluk laut raksasa yang memiliki kaki belakang kecil (vestigial) yang tidak berguna untuk menopang berat badan di darat.

questions.questionText: Based on paragraph 4, what anatomical change helped reduce drag while swimming?
options: The development of thick fur.
A perfectly streamlined body shape.
The growth of larger hind limbs.
The ability to change color for camouflage.
questions.explanation: Untuk mengurangi hambatan (drag) dalam air, tubuh cetacea berevolusi menjadi bentuk yang sangat ramping dan aerodinamis (streamlined).

questions.questionText: What is the primary difference between Odontoceti and Mysticeti?
options: Their preferred water temperature.
The way they reproduce.
Their feeding tools (teeth vs. baleen) and hunting methods.
The depth at which they can survive.
questions.explanation: Perbedaan utama terletak pada alat makan dan metode berburu: Odontoceti menggunakan gigi canggih untuk ekolokasi (berburu di kedalaman), sementara Mysticeti menggunakan lempengan baleen sebagai filter makanan.

questions.questionText: The author calls the evolution of whales a 'testament to the dynamic nature of life' to emphasize that:
options: Life is constant and never changes.
Species can undergo radical transformations over time.
Whales are the most intelligent animals in the world.
Humans must do more to protect the oceans.
questions.explanation: Frasa tersebut menekankan bahwa sejarah evolusi cetacea adalah bukti kuat bahwa spesies dapat mengalami transformasi radikal dari bentuk darat menjadi makhluk laut raksasa.

passages.title: Gothic Architecture: Reaching for the Divine
passages.content:
Gothic architecture, a style that dominated European building design from the mid-12th to the 16th century, represents a radical shift from the heavy, earthbound structures of the preceding Romanesque era. Emerging first in the Île-de-France region around Paris, the Gothic style was characterized by an unprecedented emphasis on verticality and light. This aesthetic transformation was not merely a matter of taste but was deeply rooted in the theological concept that light was a manifestation of the divine. To achieve these soaring heights and luminous interiors, medieval architects had to master several groundbreaking engineering techniques that allowed for thinner walls and larger, more intricate openings.

The most fundamental hallmark of the Gothic style is the pointed arch. Unlike the rounded arches of Romanesque buildings, which distributed weight primarily downward and necessitated thick, load-bearing walls, the pointed arch channeled the structural thrust more vertically. This efficiency meant that arches could be made much taller without increasing the width of the base, creating the sense of "reaching for heaven" that defines Gothic cathedrals. Complementing the pointed arch was the ribbed vault, a framework of arched stone ribs that supported the ceiling. By concentrating the weight of the roof onto specific points (the piers) rather than the entire wall, architects were freed from the requirement of solid, massive masonry.

However, the increased height and the concentration of weight created a new problem: lateral thrust, or the outward pressure that threatened to push the walls apart. To counter this, Gothic builders developed the flying buttress—a masonry arch that extended from the upper exterior of the wall down to a massive stone pier some distance away. These "exoskeletal" supports effectively transferred the weight of the roof away from the walls and into the ground. With the walls no longer serving as the primary load-bearing elements, they could be reduced in thickness and punctuated by gargantuan windows. These windows were often filled with stained glass, which transformed ordinary sunlight into a kaleidoscope of colors, illustrating biblical narratives for a largely illiterate population while creating a mystical, ethereal atmosphere within the sacred space.

The evolution of Gothic architecture is often categorized into distinct phases, such as the Early, High, and Late (Rayonnant or Flamboyant) periods. Each phase showed an increasing complexity in ornamentation and structural daring. For example, the Late Gothic style is noted for its "flame-like" tracery and incredibly delicate stone carvings that made the massive buildings appear almost lace-like. Iconic examples of this architectural triumph include Notre-Dame de Paris, Chartres Cathedral, and Westminster Abbey. These structures were not just places of worship; they were the tallest buildings of their time, serving as centers of urban life and symbols of the wealth and prestige of the cities that built them.

Furthermore, the impact of Gothic architecture extended beyond religious buildings. The techniques developed for cathedrals were adapted for secular structures, such as town halls, palaces, and universities, signaling the growing importance of civic institutions in late medieval society. Despite being eventually superseded by the symmetry and classical proportions of the Renaissance, the Gothic style left an indelible mark on the European landscape. Its enduring legacy can be seen in the 19th-century Gothic Revival movement, which sought to recapture the verticality and spiritual intensity of the medieval masters, proving that the desire to touch the sky through architecture remains a powerful human impulse.

questions.questionText: What is the primary focus of the passage?
options: The historical rivalry between French and English architects.
The transition from Romanesque to Renaissance building styles.
The engineering innovations and aesthetic goals of Gothic architecture.
The role of stained glass in medieval religious education.
questions.explanation: Gagasan utama (main idea) dari teks ini adalah menjelaskan inovasi teknik dan tujuan estetika yang mendasari arsitektur Gotik, mulai dari penggunaan lengkungan runcing hingga sistem penopang flying buttress.

questions.questionText: The word 'unprecedented' in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to:
options: Unexpected
Never seen before
Extremely fast
Hard to understand
questions.explanation: 'Unprecedented' berarti belum pernah terjadi atau terlihat sebelumnya. Oleh karena itu, pilihan 'Never seen before' adalah padanan yang paling tepat.

questions.questionText: According to the passage, why was 'light' so important in Gothic design?
options: It was necessary for reading religious texts.
It was believed to be a physical representation of God.
It helped keep the large cathedrals warm in winter.
It was easier to produce than artificial lighting.
questions.explanation: Teks secara eksplisit menyatakan bahwa cahaya penting karena 'light was a manifestation of the divine' (cahaya adalah manifestasi dari ilahi). Ini adalah konsep teologis yang mendasari desain Gotik.

questions.questionText: How did the pointed arch differ from the Romanesque rounded arch?
options: It was cheaper and faster to construct.
It was used only for decorative purposes rather than structural ones.
It directed weight more vertically, allowing for taller structures.
It required thicker walls to support its unique shape.
questions.explanation: Perbedaan utama adalah bahwa lengkungan runcing (pointed arch) menyalurkan dorongan struktural (structural thrust) lebih vertikal, yang memungkinkan arsitek membangun struktur yang jauh lebih tinggi tanpa perlu memperlebar dasar.

questions.questionText: What was the primary function of the 'flying buttress'?
options: To provide a platform for statues of saints.
To allow sunlight to reach the lower levels of the cathedral.
To support the walls against the outward pressure of the roof.
To channel rainwater away from the stained glass windows.
questions.explanation: 'Flying buttress' berfungsi sebagai penopang luar (exoskeletal supports) yang mentransfer berat atap menjauh dari dinding, sehingga berhasil menahan tekanan lateral (lateral thrust) agar dinding tidak roboh.

questions.questionText: The author mentions 'stained glass' in paragraph 3 to illustrate:
options: The high cost of building Gothic cathedrals.
How thinner walls enabled more artistic and light-filled interiors.
The lack of skilled stone carvers in the Middle Ages.
Why Gothic churches were often darker than Romanesque ones.
questions.explanation: Penggunaan kaca patri (stained glass) digunakan untuk menggambarkan bagaimana dinding yang lebih tipis memungkinkan jendela besar yang penuh cahaya dan seni, menciptakan suasana mistis.

questions.questionText: The word 'indelible' in paragraph 5 is closest in meaning to:
options: Invisible
Permanent
Confusing
Temporary
questions.explanation: 'Indelible' berarti permanen atau tidak dapat dihapuskan. Oleh karena itu, 'Permanent' adalah padanan yang paling tepat.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a characteristic of Late Gothic architecture?
options: Flame-like tracery in the windows.
Delicate, lace-like stone carvings.
A return to thick, solid masonry walls.
Increased complexity in ornamentation.
questions.explanation: Pilihan 'A' dan 'B' adalah ciri khas Gotik Akhir. Pilihan 'C' salah karena arsitektur Gotik justru menjauhi dinding tebal; sebaliknya, gaya Late Gothic ditandai dengan kehalusan dan kerapian.

questions.questionText: What can be inferred about Gothic architecture's influence on society?
options: It was strictly limited to the construction of cathedrals and churches.
It reflected the rising importance of non-religious, civic institutions.
It was almost entirely forgotten until the 20th century.
It caused a decline in the popularity of stained glass.
questions.explanation: Teks menyiratkan bahwa teknik Gotik tidak hanya terbatas pada gereja; teknik tersebut juga diadaptasi untuk bangunan sekuler (town halls, palaces), yang menandakan meningkatnya pentingnya institusi sipil.

questions.questionText: The author's tone in the passage can best be described as:
options: Highly critical of medieval engineering.
Objective and appreciative of architectural innovation.
Skeptical about the religious motives behind the style.
Nostalgic for the simplicity of Romanesque design.
questions.explanation: Penulis menyajikan fakta-fakta teknis dan sejarah dengan nada yang objektif, sekaligus menunjukkan apresiasi terhadap pencapaian inovasi arsitektur Gotik.

passages.title: Aquifers: The Hidden Reservoirs of the Underworld
passages.content:
While the Earth’s surface is dominated by vast oceans and winding rivers, a significant portion of the planet’s fresh water remains hidden from view, stored within complex geological formations known as aquifers. An aquifer is not an underground lake or river in the traditional sense; rather, it is a body of porous rock, gravel, or sand that is saturated with water. This groundwater resides in the tiny pore spaces between grains of sediment or within the fractures and crevices of solid rock. Accounting for roughly 30 percent of the world’s accessible fresh water, aquifers are a crucial resource for human civilization, providing water for drinking, industry, and, most importantly, the massive irrigation systems required for global agriculture.

Aquifers are generally classified into two primary types based on their geological positioning: unconfined and confined. An unconfined aquifer is one where the upper boundary is defined by the water table and is covered by permeable soil or sediment. This arrangement allows precipitation and surface water to seep directly downward through the ground to "recharge" the aquifer. In contrast, a confined aquifer is sandwiched between layers of impermeable material, such as clay or solid bedrock, known as aquitards. These impermeable layers prevent water from entering directly from the surface above. Instead, confined aquifers are typically recharged in distant areas where the geological layer eventually reaches the surface. Because the water in a confined aquifer is trapped under pressure between these layers, it may rise above the top of the aquifer when a well is drilled, creating what is known as an artesian well.

The movement of water through an aquifer is dictated by two key properties of the host material: porosity and permeability. Porosity refers to the total volume of open space within a rock or sediment, essentially determining how much water a formation can hold. However, high porosity does not always guarantee a productive aquifer. Permeability, the ability of the material to allow fluids to pass through its interconnected pores, is equally vital. For instance, clay is highly porous but has very low permeability because its tiny pores are not well-connected, making it a poor source of extractable water. In contrast, well-sorted sand or fractured limestone typically exhibits both high porosity and high permeability, making them ideal candidates for large-scale water extraction.

Despite their vast size, aquifers are not an inexhaustible resource. In many parts of the world, groundwater is being extracted at rates that far exceed the natural recharge capacity—a process known as groundwater mining. This over-extraction leads to a persistent drop in the water table, forcing farmers and municipalities to drill deeper and more expensive wells. Furthermore, the removal of water that once supported the weight of the overlying soil can lead to land subsidence. In some coastal regions, over-pumping can even cause saltwater intrusion, where seawater is pulled into the aquifer, contaminating the freshwater supply and rendering it unusable for drinking or crops.

Safeguarding these subterranean reservoirs is becoming a paramount challenge in the face of climate change and growing global populations. While surface water is susceptible to immediate pollution and evaporation, groundwater is often naturally filtered as it percolates through the earth, making it a traditionally reliable source of high-quality water. However, once an aquifer is contaminated by industrial chemicals or agricultural runoff, the cleanup process can take decades or even centuries due to the slow movement of water underground. Sustainable management, which involves monitoring recharge rates and implementing strict regulations on extraction, is therefore essential to ensure that these hidden lifelines continue to sustain life for generations to come.

questions.questionText: Which of the following best defines an 'aquifer'?
options: A large underground cavern filled with standing water.
A man-made reservoir used for storing treated drinking water.
A geological formation of porous material saturated with water.
A seasonal river that flows only after heavy rainfall.
questions.explanation: Akuifer didefinisikan sebagai formasi geologi berupa material berpori (porous material) yang jenuh dengan air (saturated with water). Ini adalah definisi teknis yang paling akurat.

questions.questionText: The word 'saturated' in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to:
options: Completely filled
Very cold
Chemically treated
Moving rapidly
questions.explanation: 'Saturated' berarti jenuh atau terisi penuh. Dalam konteks ini, air tersebut mengisi seluruh ruang pori dalam batuan atau sedimen.

questions.questionText: According to paragraph 2, how does an unconfined aquifer differ from a confined one?
options: Unconfined aquifers are only found in mountainous regions.
Unconfined aquifers allow water to seep directly from the surface above.
Confined aquifers are much larger and hold more water.
Confined aquifers are easier to recharge through rainfall.
questions.explanation: Akuifer bebas (unconfined) memiliki batas atas yang ditentukan oleh muka air tanah dan ditutupi oleh tanah atau sedimen permeabel. Ini memungkinkan presipitasi dan air permukaan meresap langsung ke akuifer.

questions.questionText: What is the significance of an 'artesian well'?
options: It is a well that requires no pump because the water is under pressure.
It is a well used specifically for mining rare minerals.
It is a type of well that only works in unconfined aquifers.
It is a well that has been contaminated by saltwater.
questions.explanation: Sumur artesis adalah sumur yang airnya naik sendiri karena tekanan hidrostatik dari akuifer tertekan (confined). Ini menunjukkan adanya tekanan di bawah lapisan batuan kedap air.

questions.questionText: Why is permeability just as important as porosity for a productive aquifer?
options: Because porosity only refers to the age of the rock.
Because water cannot be easily extracted if the pores are not interconnected.
Because high permeability prevents the aquifer from ever being contaminated.
Because permeability determines the chemical composition of the water.
questions.explanation: Meskipun porositas menentukan seberapa banyak air yang dapat ditampung, permeabilitas (kemampuan material untuk memungkinkan fluida lewat) sangat penting. Jika pori-pori tidak saling terhubung, air tidak dapat diekstraksi secara efisien.

questions.questionText: The author uses 'clay' as an example of a material that:
options: Is ideal for storing and extracting large amounts of water.
Has high porosity but low permeability.
Is the most common material found in confined aquifers.
Reacts chemically with groundwater to create toxic substances.
questions.explanation: Lempung (clay) adalah contoh material yang memiliki porositas tinggi (banyak ruang kosong) tetapi permeabilitas rendah karena pori-porinya tidak saling terhubung dengan baik, menjadikannya sumber air yang buruk untuk ekstraksi.

questions.questionText: The term 'groundwater mining' refers to:
options: The process of extracting gold and silver from underground water.
Drawing water from an aquifer faster than it can be naturally replaced.
Building tunnels to store surface water deep underground.
Using groundwater to help cool deep-sea mining equipment.
questions.explanation: 'Groundwater mining' adalah istilah yang digunakan untuk menggambarkan proses pengambilan air tanah (groundwater) pada laju yang jauh melampaui kapasitas pengisian alaminya (natural recharge capacity).

questions.questionText: Based on paragraph 4, what is a potential consequence of land subsidence?
options: An increase in the height of the water table.
The sudden appearance of new unconfined aquifers.
Damage to infrastructure as the ground sinks.
A decrease in the salinity of coastal waters.
questions.explanation: Penurunan tanah (land subsidence) terjadi ketika air ditarik keluar dari pori-pori batuan, yang menyebabkan berat lapisan atas hilang. Konsekuensinya adalah kerusakan pada infrastruktur karena tanah amblas.

questions.questionText: What is one reason why cleaning contaminated aquifers is particularly difficult?
options: The water in aquifers is too hot for most chemicals to work.
Groundwater moves very slowly, making it hard to flush out pollutants.
There are no known technologies for filtering water underground.
Confined aquifers are too deep for scientists to reach.
questions.explanation: Proses pembersihan sangat sulit karena air tanah bergerak sangat lambat di bawah permukaan. Hal ini membuat upaya untuk membilas (flush out) polutan memakan waktu puluhan tahun atau bahkan berabad-abad.

questions.questionText: What is the author's primary purpose in the passage?
options: To advocate for the privatization of all global water resources.
To explain the geology of aquifers and the importance of sustainable management.
To compare the quality of surface water with that of groundwater.
To describe the history of well-drilling technology in ancient civilizations.
questions.explanation: Tujuan utama penulis adalah menjelaskan konsep geologi akuifer (porositas dan permeabilitas) serta menekankan pentingnya pengelolaan yang berkelanjutan untuk mengatasi masalah ekstraksi berlebihan.

passages.title: The 1849 Gold Rush: A Catalyst for Californian Transformation
passages.content:
The history of the American West was irrevocably altered on January 24, 1848, when James W. Marshall, a carpenter and sawmill operator, spotted a few glittering flecks of metal in the American River at Sutter's Mill in Coloma, California. Marshall and his employer, John Sutter, attempted to keep the discovery a secret, but the news of gold spread like wildfire, first through California and then across the globe. By 1849, the trickle of fortune seekers had turned into a massive human tidal wave, launching the California Gold Rush. This era did not just define a generation of "forty-niners"; it fundamentally transformed the social, economic, and political landscape of North America, propelling a sleepy coastal territory into the forefront of the global stage.

The "forty-niners" were an incredibly diverse group of nearly 300,000 migrants who converged on California in a desperate bid for wealth. They came from every corner of the United States and from countries as far-flung as China, Australia, and various European nations. The journey itself was an grueling test of endurance. Travelers from the East Coast of the U.S. had to choose between three equally daunting routes: an arduous 18,000-mile sea voyage around the treacherous Cape Horn, a shorter but disease-ridden trek across the Isthmus of Panama, or a multi-month overland journey by covered wagon across 2,000 miles of rugged mountains and parched deserts. Those who survived the trip arrived in a land that was largely unprepared for such a massive influx of people.

San Francisco, the primary gateway to the gold fields, underwent a metamorphosis that was nothing short of miraculous. In 1848, it was a tiny settlement of about 200 residents; by 1852, it had exploded into a bustling boomtown of over 36,000 people. This rapid urbanization necessitated the immediate creation of infrastructure—roads, churches, schools, and civic governance. The sudden wealth generated by the mines fueled a boom in commerce and industry. Entrepreneurs like Levi Strauss, who realized that there was more money to be made in selling supplies to miners than in mining itself, laid the foundations for California’s future economic might. By 1850, less than two years after the initial discovery, California was admitted as the 31st state of the Union, bypassing the typical territorial phase.

However, the Gold Rush was not a story of prosperity for everyone. While a few "struck it rich," the vast majority of miners found only backbreaking labor and disappointment. As the easily accessible gold near the surface was depleted, mining became an increasingly industrialized and environmentally destructive endeavor. The introduction of hydraulic mining—using high-pressure water jets to wash away entire hillsides—resulted in massive erosion and the clogging of rivers with sediment, which caused devastating floods in the valleys below. The environmental scars from these practices are still visible in parts of the California landscape today.

Most tragically, the Gold Rush was a catastrophe for California’s Native American populations. The massive influx of settlers led to widespread displacement, the destruction of traditional food sources, and the introduction of deadly diseases. Armed conflicts between miners and indigenous groups were frequent and often ended in state-sanctioned violence. Within a few decades, the Native American population in California plummeted from an estimated 150,000 to less than 30,000. Thus, while the 1849 Gold Rush is often remembered as a period of romanticized adventure and American expansion, it remains a complex and controversial chapter in history—one that built a modern state upon a foundation of both immense wealth and profound suffering.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: The biography of James W. Marshall and his contributions to California.
The various routes taken by gold seekers to reach the American West.
The wide-ranging and complex impacts of the California Gold Rush on the region.
The technological improvements in mining during the mid-19th century.
questions.explanation: Gagasan utama teks ini adalah menjelaskan dampak luas dan kompleks dari Demam Emas California terhadap berbagai aspek kehidupan, termasuk sosial, ekonomi, politik, dan lingkungan.

questions.questionText: The word 'irrevocably' in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to:
options: Temporarily
Permanently
Mysteriously
Fortunately
questions.explanation: 'Irrevocably' berarti tidak dapat diubah kembali atau secara permanen (permanently). Oleh karena itu, 'Permanently' adalah sinonim yang paling tepat.

questions.questionText: According to paragraph 2, why was the journey to California considered a 'grueling test of endurance'?
options: Gold seekers had to swim across many dangerous rivers.
All routes involved long, difficult, and often dangerous travel.
Miners were constantly attacked by pirates and bandits.
The lack of maps made it impossible to find the gold fields.
questions.explanation: Perjalanan dianggap sebagai ujian ketahanan karena para pelancong harus memilih rute yang sangat panjang (ribuan mil), sulit, dan penuh bahaya (seperti pelayaran di Cape Horn atau perjalanan darat melintasi gurun).

questions.questionText: What does the author imply about Levi Strauss?
options: He was one of the few miners who found a massive gold deposit.
He failed in business but succeeded in politics.
He found success by providing goods to miners rather than mining himself.
He was the first person to use hydraulic mining in California.
questions.explanation: Penulis menyiratkan bahwa Levi Strauss berhasil dengan melihat peluang bisnis: ada lebih banyak uang yang bisa dihasilkan dari menjual persediaan ke penambang daripada menambang emas itu sendiri.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is true about San Francisco during the Gold Rush?
options: Its population decreased as people moved to the gold fields.
It remained a small settlement until the arrival of the railroad.
It grew from a few hundred residents to tens of thousands in a few years.
It was founded as a result of James Marshall's discovery.
questions.explanation: Teks menyatakan bahwa San Francisco berubah dari pemukiman kecil (sekitar 200 penduduk) menjadi kota yang ramai dengan lebih dari 36.000 orang hanya dalam waktu beberapa tahun.

questions.questionText: The word 'metamorphosis' in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to:
options: Conflict
Transformation
Discovery
Investment
questions.explanation: 'Metamorphosis' berarti perubahan bentuk atau transformasi (transformation). Dalam konteks San Francisco, ini merujuk pada perubahan dramatis dari pemukiman kecil menjadi kota besar.

questions.questionText: How did mining change as surface gold became harder to find?
options: Most miners gave up and returned to farming.
It became more industrialized and used methods like hydraulic mining.
Governments banned all mining to protect the environment.
Miners began using secret maps left by Spanish explorers.
questions.explanation: Ketika emas permukaan habis, penambangan menjadi lebih intensif, berindustrialisasi, dan beralih ke metode yang lebih destruktif seperti penambangan hidrolik (hydraulic mining).

questions.questionText: Based on paragraph 4, what was a negative environmental effect of hydraulic mining?
options: It caused the ground to freeze even in the summer.
It released toxic gases that killed most of the region's trees.
It led to extreme erosion and the clogging of rivers with sediment.
It caused a permanent drought in the valleys below.
questions.explanation: Penambangan hidrolik menggunakan jet air bertekanan tinggi untuk mengikis lereng. Dampak negatifnya adalah erosi ekstrem dan penyumbatan sungai oleh sedimen, yang kemudian menyebabkan banjir.

questions.questionText: What can be inferred about the impact of the Gold Rush on Native Americans?
options: They benefited from the new infrastructure and schools.
They were hired as the primary laborers in the new industrial mines.
They suffered a devastating decline in population and culture.
They successfully defended their lands against all settlers.
questions.explanation: Dapat disimpulkan bahwa masuknya pemukim secara massal menyebabkan penggusuran, penghancuran sumber makanan tradisional, dan peningkatan penyakit, yang mengakibatkan penurunan populasi Native American secara drastis.

questions.questionText: The author's attitude toward the Gold Rush can be described as:
options: Purely romanticized and celebratory.
Critical but balanced, acknowledging both growth and tragedy.
Indifferent toward the social changes it caused.
Skeptical that gold was ever actually found in California.
questions.explanation: Penulis tidak hanya memuji sisi kemakmuran dan pertumbuhan ekonomi (growth) tetapi juga menyoroti penderitaan yang mendalam (tragedy), sehingga sikapnya adalah kritis namun seimbang.

passages.title: The Ozone Layer: Earth’s Fragile Sunscreen
passages.content:
High above the clouds, between 10 and 20 miles above the surface of the Earth, lies a thin but vital shield of gas known as the ozone layer. This region of the stratosphere is characterized by relatively high concentrations of ozone (O3), a molecule composed of three oxygen atoms. While ozone at ground level is a harmful pollutant and a key component of smog, in the stratosphere it serves as the planet’s primary defense against the sun’s high-energy ultraviolet (UV) radiation. By absorbing roughly 98 percent of harmful UV rays, the ozone layer makes life on Earth possible; without it, the surface would be sterilized by radiation that causes skin cancer, cataracts, and severe damage to terrestrial and marine ecosystems.

In the mid-1970s, atmospheric scientists began to voice concerns that human activity might be inadvertently destroying this protective shield. Their focus was on a class of man-made chemicals known as chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs), which were used in refrigeration, air conditioning, aerosol sprays, and industrial cleaning solvents. For decades, CFCs were praised for being non-toxic, non-flammable, and chemically stable. However, their very stability proved to be their most dangerous trait. Unlike most pollutants that are washed out of the lower atmosphere by rain, CFCs drift slowly upward into the stratosphere, a journey that can take several years.

Once CFCs reach the stratosphere, they are subjected to intense UV radiation, which finally breaks their strong chemical bonds. This process releases highly reactive chlorine atoms. A single chlorine atom can act as a catalyst, initiating a chain reaction that destroys thousands of ozone molecules before it is eventually removed from the atmosphere. By the mid-1980s, these warnings were dramatically confirmed by the discovery of an "ozone hole"—a massive thinning of the layer over Antarctica during the southern spring. This discovery sent shockwaves through the global scientific and political communities, as it became clear that the depletion was happening much faster than earlier models had predicted.

The response to this environmental crisis was unprecedented in its speed and scope. In 1987, representatives from various nations gathered to sign the Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer. This landmark international treaty established a schedule for phasing out the production and consumption of CFCs and other ozone-depleting substances. The protocol was designed with flexibility, allowing for adjustments as new scientific evidence emerged. It is widely considered the most successful environmental agreement in history, as it achieved near-universal ratification and led to a rapid reduction in the global use of dangerous chemicals.

Today, there are clear signs that the ozone layer is beginning to heal. Atmospheric concentrations of chlorine are declining, and scientists estimate that if current trends continue, the ozone layer could return to its 1980 levels by the mid-21st century. However, the challenge is not yet over. Some of the chemicals used as replacements for CFCs, while safe for the ozone layer, are potent greenhouse gases that contribute to global warming. This highlights the complex interconnections of Earth’s atmospheric systems. The story of the ozone layer serves as both a warning of the consequences of human technological impact and a powerful example of how global cooperation can successfully address an existential environmental threat.

questions.questionText: What is the primary function of the ozone layer in the stratosphere?
options: To trap heat and keep the planet warm.
To provide oxygen for breathing at high altitudes.
To absorb the vast majority of harmful ultraviolet radiation.
To prevent airplanes from flying too high.
questions.explanation: Fungsi utama lapisan ozon adalah menyerap sekitar 98 persen radiasi ultraviolet (UV) berbahaya dari matahari, yang melindungi kehidupan di permukaan Bumi dari kerusakan seperti kanker kulit dan katarak.

questions.questionText: The word 'sterilized' in paragraph 1 is used to suggest that without the ozone layer, the Earth's surface would:
options: Become extremely clean.
Be unable to support life due to radiation.
Be covered in thick clouds.
Grow plants much faster.
questions.explanation: 'Sterilized' di sini menyiratkan bahwa radiasi UV yang tidak tersaring akan menyebabkan kerusakan parah pada permukaan Bumi, sehingga planet tersebut tidak akan mampu mendukung kehidupan.

questions.questionText: According to the passage, why were CFCs considered dangerous despite being non-toxic?
options: They were extremely expensive to manufacture.
They were highly flammable when used in aerosol sprays.
Their chemical stability allowed them to reach the stratosphere intact.
They caused immediate health problems for workers in factories.
questions.explanation: CFC dianggap berbahaya karena stabilitas kimianya yang tinggi memungkinkan mereka untuk mengambang secara perlahan ke stratosfer tanpa terurai di atmosfer bawah. Di sana, mereka melepaskan klorin reaktif.

questions.questionText: How does a single chlorine atom destroy thousands of ozone molecules?
options: By physically smothering the oxygen atoms.
By acting as a catalyst in a destructive chain reaction.
By turning into a large ozone-eating bacteria.
By absorbing all the sunlight before it reaches the ozone.
questions.explanation: Satu atom klorin bertindak sebagai katalis, yang memulai reaksi berantai destruktif di mana ia menghancurkan ribuan molekul ozon sebelum akhirnya dinetralkan.

questions.questionText: Where was the 'ozone hole' first discovered?
options: Over the Arctic Ocean.
Over the Sahara Desert.
Over the continent of Antarctica.
Above industrial cities in Europe.
questions.explanation: 'Ozone hole' pertama kali terdeteksi pada pertengahan tahun 1980-an di atas benua Antartika, menunjukkan penipisan lapisan ozon yang masif di sana.

questions.questionText: The word 'inadvertently' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to:
options: Intentionally
Unintentionally
Quickly
Successfully
questions.explanation: 'Inadvertently' berarti secara tidak sengaja atau tanpa disengaja (unintentionally). Ini menunjukkan bahwa kerusakan lapisan ozon terjadi akibat aktivitas manusia yang tidak direncanakan.

questions.questionText: The Montreal Protocol is described as 'successful' primarily because:
options: It made CFCs much cheaper to produce for everyone.
it led to the total elimination of all greenhouse gases.
It achieved global cooperation to phase out ozone-depleting chemicals.
It encouraged people to use more air conditioning.
questions.explanation: Protokol Montreal berhasil karena menciptakan kesepakatan internasional yang melibatkan berbagai negara untuk membatasi dan menghapus penggunaan bahan kimia perusak ozon secara global.

questions.questionText: Based on the passage, what is the current status of the ozone layer?
